title: 10 best herbal teas for upset stomach
intro_desc: Herbal teas for upset stomach are aromatic beverages prepared by steeping dried herbs in hot water. They provide relief from stomach discomfort due to their anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and carminative properties. Some popular herbal teas include chamomile, ginger, peppermint, and fennel. Chamomile tea soothes the digestive tract, reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation. Ginger tea is known for its effectiveness against nausea and vomiting, while peppermint and fennel teas alleviate abdominal cramps and gas. By consuming these herbal teas, individuals can experience improved digestion and reduced symptoms of stomach discomfort, leading to enhanced quality of life and overall well-being.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Peppermint teas helps with upset stomach because it contains natural oils that soothe the digestive system. The menthol in peppermint oil relaxes the muscles in the stomach, reducing cramps and spasms associated with indigestion and nausea. Additionally, peppermint's anti-inflammatory properties help to reduce inflammation in the gut, which can alleviate symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) such as bloating and abdominal pain. As a result, drinking peppermint tea can provide quick and natural relief from upset stomach discomfort.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Peppermint tea contains anti-inflammatory compounds like rosmarinic acid, which help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, alleviating symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) and other stomach issues.
Carminative properties: Peppermint oil's carminative properties help relax the muscles in the stomach and intestines, reducing spasms and cramping that can cause stomach upset, nausea, and vomiting.

Gastroprotective compounds: Peppermint tea contains gastroprotective compounds like menthol, which help protect the stomach lining from acid damage and inflammation, reducing the risk of ulcers and gastritis.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in menthol and other oils, peppermint roots help to ease digestive discomfort and reduce inflammation in the gut.
Bark: The bark of the peppermint plant contains a high concentration of menthol, making it an effective remedy for soothing indigestion and calming upset stomachs.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried peppermint leaves or two teaspoons of fresh peppermint leaves per cup.
Heat water to a temperature between 195°f and 205°f and let it cool for one minute.
Add the measured peppermint leaves to the cooled water and allow it steep for five to seven minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup or teapot and discard solids.
Serve hot or chilled, sweetened with honey if desired, after cooling for at least 10 minutes.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm teas helps with upset stomach because of its soothing and calming properties. The tea's flavonoids and volatile oils work together to ease digestive discomfort, reducing inflammation and spasms in the digestive tract. This can provide fast relief from symptoms such as bloating, cramps, and nausea, helping to settle an upset stomach and promote a sense of calm and relaxation.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: Lemon balm tea helps to relax the muscles in the stomach and intestines, reducing spasms and cramps caused by digestive issues like irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).
Carminative properties: Lemon balm tea has a calming effect on the digestive system, helping to prevent the formation of gas and flatulence, which can contribute to upset stomach and discomfort.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Lemon balm tea contains compounds that have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate symptoms of conditions like gastritis or gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD).
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in bioactive compounds, the roots are used to calm digestive issues and soothe irritable bowel syndrome.
Leaves: The leaves contain a high concentration of rosmarinic acid, which helps to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms of indigestion.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried melissa officinalis leaves measuring approximately one teaspoon per cup.
Rinse the leaves with filtered water to remove any impurities or debris gently.
Combine the rinsed leaves with boiling water in a heat-resistant tea infuser or teapot steeping for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth discarding solids immediately.
Sweeten to taste if desired and serve hot or let cool to enjoy at room temperature for up to 24 hours.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: German chamomile teas helps with upset stomach because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the digestive tract. The flavonoids and apigenin present in the tea calm down the muscles in the stomach, reducing cramps and spasms that can cause discomfort. Additionally, chamomile's gentle sedative effects help to relax the digestive system, allowing food to move through the body more smoothly and reducing symptoms of indigestion and bloating.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Chamomile's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as apigenin and luteolin, help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, which can alleviate symptoms of upset stomach, including bloating and discomfort.

Gastroprotective properties: Chamomile's flavonoids and terpenoids have been shown to protect the mucous membranes in the stomach and small intestine from damage caused by stress, acid reflux, or other irritants, reducing the risk of gastrointestinal bleeding and ulcers.

Spasmolytic properties: The volatile oils present in chamomile tea, such as borneol and bisabolol, have a calming effect on smooth muscles in the digestive tract, helping to relax and soothe spasms and cramps associated with upset stomach, including irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).
remedy_parts: Roots: Used for its calming and soothing properties, roots help to ease digestive discomfort and reduce inflammation in the stomach.
Flowers: Rich in apigenin, an antioxidant that relaxes the muscles in the stomach and reduces spasms, flowers are a key ingredient in chamomile tea for upset stomach relief.
remedy_recipe: Gather one tablespoon of dried chamomile flowers or two tablespoons of fresh flowers for every eight ounces of water.
Heat water in a tea kettle or pot to a temperature between 195 and 205 degrees fahrenheit.
Pour the hot water over the chamomile flowers and let it steep for five to seven minutes to allow flavors to meld.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup or teapot to remove solids.
Let the tea cool to a comfortable drinking temperature and enjoy within 24 hours of preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Mugwort teas helps with upset stomach because its unique blend of compounds, including bitter acids and volatile oils, has a soothing effect on the digestive system. The bitter taste stimulates saliva production and increases gastric juices, which can help to alleviate nausea and cramps. Additionally, mugwort's anti-inflammatory properties may reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, calming symptoms such as bloating and discomfort associated with upset stomachs.
remedy_properties: Soothing and anti-inflammatory effects: Mugwort's flavonoids and terpenes have been shown to reduce inflammation and soothe irritated tissues in the digestive tract, helping to alleviate symptoms of upset stomach.

Antispasmodic properties: The essential oils present in mugwort tea, such as thujone and camphor, can help relax smooth muscle contractions in the digestive tract, reducing cramps, spasms, and discomfort associated with upset stomach.

Gut-healing and antimicrobial effects: Mugwort's bioactive compounds have been found to have antimicrobial properties, which can help combat gut imbalances and infections that may contribute to upset stomach symptoms, while also promoting the growth of beneficial gut bacteria.
remedy_parts: Roots: Mugwort roots have a soothing effect on the digestive system, helping to calm nausea and indigestion.
Leaves: The leaves contain a high concentration of mucilage, which helps to protect the stomach lining from irritation and inflammation.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried mugwort leaves and flowers according to sustainable foraging practices.
Combine one teaspoon of dried mugwort with one cup boiling water in a tea infuser or heat-resistant cup.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes, allowing the herbs to release their medicinal properties into the liquid.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth and discard the solids retaining only the clear liquid.
Drink the mugwort tea immediately while still warm, taking small sips throughout the day as needed for relaxation and sedation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Fennel teas helps with upset stomach because its active compounds, including fiber and volatile oils, work to soothe and calm the digestive tract. The tea's natural properties help to reduce inflammation, ease cramps, and alleviate symptoms of indigestion and bloating, allowing for a more comfortable and relaxed digestive process. Additionally, fennel's gentle stimulation of digestive enzymes can also help to improve digestion and relieve discomfort.
remedy_properties: Carminative properties: Fennel's carminative properties help to release gas from the digestive system, providing relief from bloating and discomfort in the abdomen. This is because fennel contains compounds such as estragole and anethole, which have been shown to reduce spasms and relieve cramps in the intestines.

Antispasmodic properties: Fennel's antispasmodic properties help to relax the muscles of the digestive tract, reducing cramping, spasms, and pain associated with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) and other stomach conditions. This is because fennel contains flavonoids and lignans, which have been shown to inhibit smooth muscle contraction and relieve spasticity.

Digestive enzyme stimulation: Fennel's essential oils and phytochemicals stimulate the production of digestive enzymes in the small intestine, improving nutrient absorption and reducing symptoms of indigestion, bloating, and discomfort. This is because fennel contains a compound called anethole, which has been shown to increase the activity of digestive enzymes and improve gut motility.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the fennel plant contain natural oils and compounds that help soothe and calm the digestive system, reducing symptoms of upset stomach.
Seeds: Fennel seeds have been traditionally used to treat indigestion, bloating, and gas due to their carminative properties, which help to ease flatulence.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried fennel leaves foeniculum vulgare one cup.
Chop the leaves into small pieces three teaspoons.
Combine chopped leaves with boiling water one quart four minutes.
Strain the liquid and discard solids let it cool completely.
Store the tea in airtight container refrigerate for up to five days.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Spearmint teas helps with upset stomach because it contains compounds that have a soothing effect on the digestive system. The tea's menthol content can help to relax the muscles in the stomach and reduce inflammation, alleviating symptoms of indigestion and nausea. Additionally, spearmint's natural carminative properties can help to expel gas and relieve bloating, providing relief from discomfort and cramps associated with upset stomachs. Overall, spearmint teas can help to calm and quiet the digestive system, promoting a sense of relief and comfort.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: Spearmint tea helps relax the muscles in the digestive tract, reducing cramps and spasms that can cause nausea, bloating, and discomfort.

Digestive enzyme stimulation: Spearmint contains compounds that stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, helping to break down food more efficiently and alleviate symptoms like indigestion and gas.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Spearmint tea's anti-inflammatory compounds may help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, which can contribute to conditions like irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) and other stomach upsets.
remedy_parts: Roots: Spearmint roots contain compounds that help soothe digestive issues and alleviate symptoms of upset stomach, such as bloating and discomfort.
Leaves: The leaves of the spearmint plant have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the stomach and intestines, which can contribute to an upset stomach.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh spearmint leaves by cutting off stem ends at the soil line to encourage new growth.
Rinse 20-30 grams of harvested spearmint leaves with cold running water to remove dirt and debris.
Combine rinsed spearmint leaves with 200ml of boiling water in a medium-sized heat-resistant glass measuring cup or teapot.
Allow the mixture to steep for 5-7 minutes, allowing the menthol and flavonoids to infuse into the liquid.
Strain the tea by pouring it through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a large mug or teapot, discarding solids.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Licorice teas helps with upset stomach because its active compounds, glycyrrhizin and flavonoids, possess anti-inflammatory properties that soothe and calm the digestive tract. The tea's mucilages also help to coat and protect the stomach lining, reducing irritation and inflammation caused by acid reflux, heartburn, or spicy foods. This natural remedy can provide fast relief from discomfort, cramping, and bloating associated with an upset stomach, promoting a sense of calm and well-being.
remedy_properties: Glycyrrhizin: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help soothe digestive issues such as heartburn, acid reflux, and gastritis by reducing inflammation in the esophagus and stomach.
Flavonoids: These antioxidants have antispasmodic effects, which can help relax the muscles of the digestive tract, reducing cramps, spasms, and bloating associated with upset stomach.

Triterpenes: These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can help reduce inflammation and kill harmful bacteria in the gut that may contribute to digestive issues such as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).
remedy_parts: Roots: Licorice roots have anti-inflammatory properties and help soothe digestive issues, making them an effective ingredient in medicinal teas for upset stomach.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzome of the licorice plant contains glycyrrhizin, a compound that has been shown to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried glycyrrhiza glabra root powder per 8 oz water.
Heat filtered water to a boil in a medium saucepan.
Pour boiling water over the measured herb and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a teapot.
Serve warm or chilled, garnished with fresh mint leaves if desired.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Eureka teas helps with upset stomach because of its unique blend of soothing herbs that calm digestive issues. The peppermint, chamomile, and lemon balm in Eureka teas work together to relax the muscles in the digestive tract, reducing cramps and spasms that can cause discomfort. Additionally, these herbs have natural anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce inflammation and swelling in the stomach, providing relief from bloating and nausea. As a result, Eureka teas can provide quick and effective relief for upset stomachs.
remedy_properties: Ginger: Ginger has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce nausea and alleviate digestive discomfort by reducing inflammation in the stomach lining.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in saponins and tannins, which help to soothe and calm digestive issues, making it effective in treating upset stomach.
Rhyzomes: Known for its anti-inflammatory properties, it helps to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate symptoms of upset stomach.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh lemons at peak ripeness for optimal flavor and aroma extraction.
Wash the lemons thoroughly with filtered water to remove any impurities or dirt.
Dry the lemons in a clean, dry area away from direct sunlight for 7-10 days.
Blend 5 grams of dried lemon peel with 250 milliliters of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a teapot or cup and enjoy immediately.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Rosemary teas helps with upset stomach because its natural oils, such as bornyl acetate, have a calming effect on the digestive system. The tea's antispasmodic properties help to relax the muscles in the stomach and intestines, reducing cramps and spasms that can cause discomfort. Additionally, rosemary's anti-inflammatory properties may help soothe the lining of the gut, reducing inflammation and irritation that can contribute to upset stomach symptoms such as bloating, gas, and nausea.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Rosemary tea contains anti-inflammatory compounds like camphor, bornyl acetate, andborneol, which help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, alleviating symptoms of upset stomach such as cramps and bloating.

Antispasmodic properties: The volatile oils present in rosemary tea, including rosmarinic acid, have a relaxing effect on the muscles of the digestive tract, reducing spasms and cramps associated with upset stomach.

Carminative action: Rosemary tea has been traditionally used as a carminative, meaning it helps to eliminate gas and reduce bloating by stimulating digestion and improving the movement of food through the digestive system.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and essential oils, rosemary roots help soothe digestive issues and calm stomach discomfort.
Leaves: The leaves of the rosemary plant contain rosmarinic acid, which has anti-inflammatory properties that can aid in reducing inflammation and relieving stomach pain.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried rosemary leaves for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Heat filtered water in a pot until it reaches a rolling boil for two minutes preparation time.
Add the measured rosemary leaves to the boiling water and let steep for five minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup or teapot using gentle pressure.
Discard the solids and serve hot, allowing the tea to infuse for an additional two minutes.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marjoram teas helps with upset stomach because it contains natural compounds that soothe and calm the digestive system. The warm, aromatic brew gently relaxes the muscles in the stomach, reducing cramps and spasms associated with nausea and indigestion. Additionally, marjoram's anti-inflammatory properties help to reduce inflammation in the gut, which can contribute to a range of digestive issues, including bloating, gas, and stomach discomfort. As a result, sipping on a cup of marjoram tea can bring quick relief from upset stomach symptoms.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: Marjoram tea helps to relax and calm the muscles in the digestive tract, reducing cramps, spasms, and discomfort associated with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) and other stomach upsets.

Carminative properties: Marjoram's carminative properties help to release trapped gas and reduce bloating, flatulence, and discomfort caused by swallowing air or eating certain foods that are difficult to digest.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Marjoram tea contains flavonoids and terpenes that have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help to soothe and reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, alleviating symptoms such as diarrhea, constipation, and stomach pain.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the marjoram plant have been traditionally used to calm digestive issues and soothe upset stomachs due to their anti-inflammatory properties.
Seeds: Marjoram seeds are used in herbal teas to help reduce symptoms of indigestion and alleviate cramps, thanks to their carminative and antispasmodic properties.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried marjoram leaves from your garden or store-bought sources measuring 2 tablespoons per serving.
Rinse the marjoram leaves with cool running water to remove any dirt or debris and pat them dry gently with paper towels.
Heat 8 ounces of filtered water in a medium saucepan over medium heat until it reaches a gentle simmer for 5-7 minutes.
Add the rinsed and dried marjoram leaves to the hot water and steep for 5-10 minutes or according to desired strength and flavor profile.
Strain the tea into cups using a tea infuser or a piece of cheesecloth and serve immediately with optional sweeteners like honey or sugar to taste.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat upset stomach with herbal teas is by selecting teas with soothing properties. Peppermint and ginger teas are well-known for their calming effects on the digestive system. Peppermint contains menthol, which can help relax gastrointestinal muscles, while ginger is known to reduce inflammation and nausea. Chamomile tea is another excellent choice due to its anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which can help alleviate stomach cramps and spasms. To maximize benefits, sip these teas slowly, allowing the warm liquid to soothe your digestive tract.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to upset stomach that are treated with herbal teas include indigestion, bloating, and gastric discomfort. Peppermint tea is commonly used to soothe digestive issues, as it helps to relax the gastrointestinal muscles and improve the flow of bile. Ginger tea is another popular choice, as it contains anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce inflammation in the stomach and alleviate nausea. Chamomile tea is also known for its calming effects on the digestive system, and can help to reduce stomach cramps and spasms. Licorice root tea can help to heal the stomach lining and soothe heartburn, while fennel tea can aid in the expulsion of gas and reduce bloating. However, it is important to consult with a healthcare professional before using herbal teas as a treatment for any ailment, especially for those with underlying medical conditions or those who are taking medication.
